topic: A coach has to cut a player from the team
raw_output:
[story 1]

The silence in the locker room was deafening, amplifying the frantic drumming in my chest. I stared at the clipboard in my hand, the name scrawled in red ink a stark accusation. Liam. He was the heart of this squad, the one who always brought that spark, that undeniable fire to every practice. Saying it felt like swallowing glass. I could feel the heat rising in my neck, a prickly sensation that had nothing to do with the room temperature. When I finally spoke, my voice came out thin, reedy. "This was a difficult decision," I managed, the words tasting like ash. I watched him pack his bag, his shoulders slumped, and the sight twisted something tight in my stomach. I wanted to reach out, to smooth the furrow in his brow, but I kept my hands clenched at my sides, rooted to the spot by the weight of what I’d just done.

[story 2]

I kept catching my reflection in the darkened window of the gym. My face looked strained, pulled tight around the edges. Every time I looked, I saw the shadow of his disappointed gaze, the way his eyes had flickered away when I’d delivered the news. It wasn't the decision itself that was the hardest part; it was the aftermath. I paced the empty floor, the squeak of my sneakers echoing hollowly. My jaw was clenched so tightly I could feel the strain in my molars. I kept replaying the conversation in my head, dissecting every syllable, searching for a mistake I could have avoided. A deep, unsettling ache settled low in my abdomen, a heavy, dull pressure that made breathing shallow. I needed to fix it, to rewind the tape, but the tape was already running, and the consequences were already set in stone.

[story 3]

The rain lashed against the stadium windows, mirroring the turmoil inside me. I stood by the doorway, watching the empty field where Liam used to dominate. The air felt thick, suffocating. I tried to focus on the logistics—the paperwork, the schedule adjustments—anything to distract from the raw feeling churning beneath my ribs. Each time I moved, there was a slight tremor in my hands, a physical manifestation of the internal conflict. I remember thinking, quite sharply, about the easy camaraderie we shared, about the trust he placed in me. That memory stung. I walked back to the bench, my steps slow and deliberate, feeling every muscle tense. I just wanted to disappear, to become invisible, because right now, being seen felt like an unbearable exposure.
